description:
fz-ul4.23.4.6 introduced the Backend trait as the keystone for JIT/AOT factoring. fz-ul4.23.6 used it to add an AOT driver. But the pipeline THROUGH the trait is duplicated: `compile()` and `compile_aot()` are ~90% the same code, side by side.

Audit (today):

| stage                          | compile()            | compile_aot()                              |
|--------------------------------|----------------------|--------------------------------------------|
| Schema construction            | identical            | identical                                  |
| Backend construction           | JitBackend           | AotBackend                                 |
| declare_runtime_symbols<M>     | identical            | identical                                  |
| User fn declarations           | Linkage::Local       | Linkage::Export                            |
| tuple_arities / bs scan        | identical            | identical                                  |
| user_schemas / tuple_schema_ids| identical            | identical                                  |
| ir_typer + rewrite_vec_kinds   | identical            | identical                                  |
| Per-fn compile_fn loop         | identical            | identical                                  |
| Finalize                       | finalize_definitions + fn_ptrs HashMap | emit object + dispatch/frame_size/atom/main shims |

The architectural insight: a JIT with no deoptimization (fz never speculates — strong types make IC/speculation unnecessary) is structurally identical to AOT. The only legit differences are:

  1. *When* compilation fires (lazy vs eager) — orthogonal to the trait.
  2. *Where* the compiled bytes land (RAM fn-ptr table vs object file).
  3. How per-program metadata is carried (Rust HashMap vs Cranelift data symbols emitted alongside code).

(3) collapses on inspection: fz_aot_dispatch/fz_aot_frame_size/atom_blob are the same content as CompiledModule.fn_ptrs/frame_sizes/atom_names, just encoded differently.

## Deliverable

Backend trait grows to capture the legit variation points:

```rust
pub trait Backend {
    type Module: cranelift_module::Module;
    type Output;
    fn module_mut(&mut self) -> &mut Self::Module;
    /// Linkage applied to user fns. JIT: Local; AOT: Export.
    fn fn_linkage(&self) -> cranelift_module::Linkage;
    /// Emit per-program metadata carriers (dispatch / frame_size /
    /// atom blob / C main shim). JIT no-ops; AOT emits Cranelift
    /// data + fns so the linker can resolve them.
    fn emit_metadata_carriers(
        &mut self,
        fbctx: &mut FunctionBuilderContext,
        meta: &CompiledMetadata,
    ) -> Result<(), CodegenError>;
    /// Finalize the backend into its Output. JIT: finalize_definitions
    /// + build fn_ptrs → CompiledModule. AOT: object emit → AotArtifact.
    fn finalize(self, meta: CompiledMetadata) -> Result<Self::Output, CodegenError>;
}
```

`pub fn compile_with_backend<B: Backend>(module: &Module, backend: B) -> Result<B::Output, CodegenError>` runs the shared pipeline and returns whatever the backend finalizes to.

`compile()` becomes a thin wrapper:
```rust
pub fn compile(module: &Module) -> Result<CompiledModule, CodegenError> {
    compile_with_backend(module, JitBackend::new())
}
```

`compile_aot()` becomes equally thin:
```rust
pub fn compile_aot(module: &Module, obj_name: &str) -> Result<AotArtifact, CodegenError> {
    compile_with_backend(module, AotBackend::new(obj_name))
}
```

The AOT-specific dispatch/frame_size/atom-blob/main emission moves into AotBackend::emit_metadata_carriers. The JIT-specific finalize moves into JitBackend::finalize.

## What this proves

After unification, "JIT vs AOT" stops being a parity story and becomes a routing decision. Any optimization landed in the shared pipeline applies to both automatically; any bug in compile_fn is one bug, not two. The fixture matrix's `# paths: jit, aot` becomes a test of two invocation surfaces over identical generated code, not two independent code paths.

## Acceptance

- compile() and compile_aot() are each ~10-20 lines, both delegating to compile_with_backend.
- AotBackend owns the dispatch/frame_size/atom_blob/main emission via emit_metadata_carriers.
- JitBackend's emit_metadata_carriers is a no-op (the data lives in the returned CompiledModule's hashmaps).
- Linkage difference handled via Backend::fn_linkage.
- cargo test green; every existing fixture runs identically through both backends.
- Diff stat: code shrinks net (target: ~400 lines net removed from ir_codegen.rs).

## Sharpens "three-path parity"

After this lands, the matrix can either:

A. Keep three columns (jit / interp / aot) and reframe them as *invocation paths*: lazy in-process compile, AST interp, eager build + link. Same generated code in two columns; different code in the third.

B. Collapse to two columns (compiled / interp) with `# paths: compiled` running both jit and aot drivers under the hood. More honest, less ceremonial.

(B) is the design recommendation but (A) is a smaller move and preserves observability. The ticket should land (B) as a follow-up.
title: Unify compile() and compile_aot() through Backend trait
